schools.summary: The river of the water of life: 'and he shewed me a pure river of water of life, clear as crystal, proceeding out of the throne of God and of the Lamb' — the angel showing John a river of the water of life, pure and clear as crystal (the figure of the abundant, life-giving grace and blessedness of the new creation, Ezekiel 47:1; Zechariah 14:8; John 4:14, the river of life flowing through the holy city); 'water of life' signifying the fullness of eternal life, grace, and the satisfaction of the soul (the river the perpetual supply of life and joy to the redeemed, whether the Spirit, or the grace and blessedness flowing from God); 'proceeding out of the throne of God and of the Lamb' the source (the life and blessedness of the redeemed flowing from the throne, from God and Christ together) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

Bede (with Cyril): the river of the water of life proceeding from the throne of God and of the Lamb is the grace of the Holy Spirit, or the stream of eternal life, flowing from God and Christ to his people — the perpetual, life-giving supply of grace and blessedness that waters the holy city and refreshes the redeemed forever, its source the throne of God and the Lamb together.

themes: a pure river of water of life, clear as crystal, proceeding out of the throne of God and of the Lamb
the river of the water of life from the throne
the abundant, eternal life and grace of God streaming to the redeemed
the river flowing from God and the Lamb

schools.summary: The tree of life: 'in the midst of the street of it, and on either side of the river, was there the tree of life, which bare twelve manner of fruits, and yielded her fruit every month: and the leaves of the tree were for the healing of the nations' — the tree of life (Genesis 2:9; 3:22-24) growing on both sides of the river, in the city's street (the tree, lost in Eden, restored in the new creation, whether one tree or a row of trees on each bank, the figure of restored, abundant life, drawn from Ezekiel 47:12); 'twelve manner of fruits (twelve crops), and yielded her fruit every month' (the tree bearing fruit continually, a fresh crop every month, the figure of the perpetual, unfailing supply of life and nourishment to the redeemed, never barren, ever fruitful); 'the leaves of the tree were for the healing (therapeia) of the nations' (the leaves serving for the healing/health of the nations, whether the figure of the perpetual health and well-being of the redeemed — no more sickness or curse — or of the saving, restoring grace, the symbol of the perfect, restored wholeness of the redeemed nations) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Cyril: the leaves of the tree of life for the healing of the nations signify the life-giving, restoring grace that flows to the redeemed peoples — the perpetual health and wholeness of those who eat of the tree, the curse of Eden reversed and the nations healed; the tree of life, lost in paradise, restored to the redeemed in the heavenly city, its fruit and leaves the perpetual ministry of eternal life.

themes: the tree of life, which bare twelve manner of fruits, and yielded her fruit every month
the leaves of the tree were for the healing of the nations
the tree of life with its twelve fruits and healing leaves
the perpetual life, nourishment, and wholeness of the redeemed

schools.summary: The curse removed and service rendered: 'and there shall be no more curse: but the throne of God and of the Lamb shall be in it; and his servants shall serve him' — the curse pronounced at the fall (Genesis 3:17) wholly removed in the new creation ('no more curse,' the word also meaning 'no more accursed thing,' the figure of the perfect blessedness and holiness of the new order, the curse of sin abolished); 'the throne of God and of the Lamb shall be in it' (God and the Lamb reigning in the city, the throne, the seat of God's presence and rule, in the holy city, God and Christ immediately present and reigning among the redeemed); 'his servants shall serve him' (the redeemed serving God and the Lamb, their blessed estate not idleness but glad, perpetual service and worship, the servants of God serving him forever in the curse-free city, perfect, joyful service in his presence) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: there shall be no more curse: but the throne of God and of the Lamb shall be in it
his servants shall serve him
the curse removed and God's servants serving him
the curse of the fall abolished, the redeemed serving God forever

schools.summary: The beatific vision: 'and they shall see his face; and his name shall be in their foreheads' — the redeemed shall see God's face (the beatific vision, the highest blessedness, Matthew 5:8; 1 John 3:2; Psalm 17:15; 1 Corinthians 13:12, the redeemed beholding God directly, face to face, the vision denied to mortals, Exodus 33:20, granted to the glorified, the supreme joy and consummation of the redeemed); 'his name shall be in their foreheads' (God's name on their foreheads, 3:12; 14:1, in contrast to the beast's mark, 13:16, the mark of his ownership and their belonging wholly to him, the redeemed sealed as God's own, bearing his name openly) — Barnes, Robertson.

Cyril: the redeemed shall see God's face — the beatific vision, the perfect, direct communion with God that is the crown of the blessed state; to behold God face to face, with his name on their foreheads as the seal of his ownership, is the supreme and final joy of the saints, the vision of God that surpasses all and satisfies forever.

themes: they shall see his face; and his name shall be in their foreheads
the redeemed seeing God's face and bearing his name
the beatific vision, the supreme joy of the redeemed
the perfect communion with God and the seal of his ownership

schools.summary: The eternal day and reign: 'and there shall be no night there; and they need no candle, neither light of the sun; for the Lord God giveth them light: and they shall reign for ever and ever' — no night and no need of lamp or sun (resuming 21:23-25, the created and artificial lights superseded, perpetual day without darkness in the holy city); 'for the Lord God giveth them light' (God himself the light of the redeemed, 21:23; Isaiah 60:19, his presence and glory illumining them forever, no other light needed where God shines); 'they shall reign for ever and ever' (the redeemed reigning forever, 3:21; 5:10; Daniel 7:18, 27, not a thousand years only but unto the ages of ages, the saints sharing Christ's eternal reign, the consummate, endless royalty of the redeemed in the new creation) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: there shall be no night there; and they need no candle, neither light of the sun; for the Lord God giveth them light
they shall reign for ever and ever
the eternal day and the everlasting reign of the redeemed
the perpetual light of God's presence and the endless reign of the saints

schools.summary: The words affirmed: 'and he said unto me, These sayings are faithful and true: and the Lord God of the holy prophets sent his angel to shew unto his servants the things which must shortly be done' — the angel affirming (beginning the epilogue) that the words of the prophecy are faithful (trustworthy) and true (genuine), the whole revelation sealed as God's reliable word (the assurance of its certainty, as 21:5; 19:9); 'the Lord God of the holy prophets sent his angel to shew unto his servants' (the same God who inspired the prophets of old having sent his angel to reveal these things, the revelation standing in the line of the prophetic word, from the God of the prophets, its authority divine); 'the things which must shortly (soon) be done' (echoing the opening, 1:1, the events to come to pass soon/quickly, whether of imminent fulfillment or of the certainty and nearness in God's reckoning, the prophecy concerning things shortly to be accomplished) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: These sayings are faithful and true: and the Lord God of the holy prophets sent his angel to shew unto his servants the things which must shortly be done
the affirmation that the sayings are faithful and true
the revelation authenticated as God's reliable, near-fulfilled word
the God of the prophets sending his angel

schools.summary: The promise to come quickly: 'behold, I come quickly: blessed is he that keepeth the sayings of the prophecy of this book' — Christ himself declaring 'I come quickly' (the first of three in this chapter, vv.7, 12, 20) and pronouncing blessed the one who keeps (heeds, obeys) the words of the prophecy; 'I come quickly' marking the imminence (Christ's coming near and sudden, 'quickly' marking the swiftness and certainty of his coming, the perpetual expectation of the church, whether his coming in judgment, in providence, or at the last day); 'blessed is he that keepeth the sayings of the prophecy of this book' the sixth beatitude (echoing 1:3, blessed the one who keeps — treasures and obeys — the words of the prophecy, the book to be heeded not merely read, the obedient blessed) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Behold, I come quickly: blessed is he that keepeth the sayings of the prophecy of this book
Christ's promise to come quickly and the blessing on the keeper
the swift coming and the beatitude on the obedient
the book to be heeded and obeyed

schools.summary: John's attestation and worship: 'and I John saw these things, and heard them. And when I had heard and seen, I fell down to worship before the feet of the angel which shewed me these things' — John attesting that he saw and heard these things (the personal testimony of the seer, the eyewitness testimony of the apostle sealing the truth of the revelation), and, overwhelmed, falling to worship the angel (as 19:10, the second instance of John's mistaken worship); 'I John saw these things, and heard them' (John personally attesting the reality of the visions); 'I fell down to worship before the feet of the angel' (overcome by the glory of the revelation, John again, as 19:10, falling to worship the angel, the repeated error occasioning the angel's renewed rebuke, v.9, the impulse to worship the glorious messenger which must be redirected to God) — Barnes, Robertson, JFB.

themes: I John saw these things, and heard them. And when I had heard and seen, I fell down to worship before the feet of the angel
John falling to worship the angel
the eyewitness attestation of the apostle and his repeated error
the impulse to worship the messenger

schools.summary: The angel refuses worship: 'then saith he unto me, See thou do it not: for I am thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren the prophets, and of them which keep the sayings of this book: worship God' — the angel forbidding the worship (as 19:10), declaring himself a fellow servant of John, the prophets, and all who keep the words of the book (he a creature, a servant like them, not to be worshiped); 'I am thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren the prophets, and of them which keep the sayings of this book' (the angel standing with John, the prophets, and the obedient as a fellow servant of God, angels and saints alike God's servants, none to be worshiped but God); 'worship God' (the angel directing all worship to God alone, the repeated lesson, 19:10, that worship belongs to God not to angels or any creature, the rebuke of angel-worship and the affirmation of worship due to God only) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: See thou do it not: for I am thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren the prophets... worship God
the angel refusing worship and directing it to God
the rebuke of angel-worship
worship belonging to God alone

schools.summary: The prophecy not sealed: 'and he saith unto me, Seal not the sayings of the prophecy of this book: for the time is at hand' — the angel bidding John not to seal (keep secret) the words of the prophecy (in contrast to Daniel, who was told to seal his words, Daniel 12:4, 9, for the time was distant, John to leave the prophecy open, for the time is near); 'seal not' (the prophecy to remain open, accessible, and proclaimed, unlike Daniel's sealed book this revelation for present use, the words to be made known not hidden); 'for the time is at hand' (the reason the prophecy is not sealed: the time of its fulfillment near, 1:3, the events not distant but at hand, the nearness of the fulfillment making the open proclamation fitting, the prophecy for the church now) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Seal not the sayings of the prophecy of this book: for the time is at hand
the command not to seal the prophecy
the prophecy left open, unlike Daniel's sealed book
the nearness of the fulfillment

schools.summary: The fixity of moral states: 'he that is unjust, let him be unjust still: and he which is filthy, let him be filthy still: and he that is righteous, let him be righteous still: and he that is holy, let him be holy still' — at the end, each one's moral state fixed (the unjust and filthy, the righteous and holy, remaining as they are, the declaration of the permanence of character at the consummation, when the time of change is past); the declaration referring to 'the scenes beyond' the present life (to the time when probation is over and character is fixed, there being no fixed, unchangeable condition in this world but at the end each remaining in his state, the solemn truth that the moral condition is sealed at the consummation — not a license to sin but a declaration of the irreversible fixity of states after the judgment); the righteous and holy remaining so forever (their state too fixed in blessedness, the permanence of the redeemed in righteousness as of the lost in sin, the great divide made final) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: He that is unjust, let him be unjust still... and he that is holy, let him be holy still
the fixity of moral states at the end
the moral condition sealed and irreversible at the consummation
the great divide made final

schools.summary: Christ's coming with reward: 'and, behold, I come quickly; and my reward is with me, to give every man according as his work shall be' — Christ again declaring 'I come quickly' (the second of three, vv.7, 12, 20), bringing his reward with him to render to every man according to his work (the promise of his swift coming and the just recompense of all, Isaiah 40:10; Matthew 16:27); 'my reward is with me' (Christ bringing the reward and recompense at his coming, rendering to each his due, the rewards and judgments in his hand); 'to give every man according as his work shall be' (the principle of judgment by works, 20:12-13; Romans 2:6; Matthew 16:27, each recompensed according to his deeds, the faithful rewarded, the wicked judged, the just retribution at Christ's coming) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: behold, I come quickly; and my reward is with me, to give every man according as his work shall be
Christ's coming with his reward according to works
the swift coming and the just recompense of all
the judgment by works at Christ's coming

schools.summary: Christ as Alpha and Omega: 'I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end, the first and the last' — Christ proclaiming himself Alpha and Omega (the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet), the beginning and the end, the first and the last (1:8, 17; 21:6; Isaiah 44:6, the title of eternity and supremacy, Christ the eternal one, the origin and goal of all); 'Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end' (the comprehensive title of God, 21:6, here claimed by Christ, he the first and the last, eternal, encompassing all, the title affirming Christ's deity and his sovereignty over all history); 'the first and the last' (the title applied to Jehovah, Isaiah 44:6; 48:12, here Christ's, the threefold designation emphatically affirming Christ's eternity and supreme deity, he who comes, v.12, the eternal Lord) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end, the first and the last
Christ's self-proclamation as Alpha and Omega
Christ the eternal one, the origin and goal of all
the divine title of Jehovah claimed by Christ

schools.summary: The seventh beatitude and its variant: 'blessed are they that do his commandments, that they may have right to the tree of life, and may enter in through the gates into the city' — the seventh and last beatitude of Revelation. A textual crux: the Received Text (KJV) reads 'Blessed are they that do his commandments' (blessed the obedient who keep God's commandments — Gill, Clarke, Wesley); the critical text, following the better manuscripts, reads 'Blessed are they that wash their robes' (hoi plynontes tas stolas auton — blessed those who wash their robes in the Lamb's blood, 7:14 — Ellicott, Robertson, Swete adopting it, the two readings differing by similar-looking Greek words). Either way the blessing is held out to the faithful (the obedient, or the cleansed); 'that they may have right (exousia — authority, access) to the tree of life, and may enter in through the gates into the city' — the blessed having access to the tree of life (v.2; 2:7) and entrance through the gates into the holy city, the reward of the faithful the tree of life and the city of God.

debates.question: Does Revelation 22:14 read 'they that do his commandments' or 'they that wash their robes'?

sides.position: 'They that do his commandments' — the Received Text / KJV / Majority reading
'They that wash their robes' (in the Lamb's blood) — the critical text, following the better manuscripts

themes: Blessed are they that do his commandments, that they may have right to the tree of life
may enter in through the gates into the city
the blessing on the obedient (the textual crux: 'do his commandments' vs 'wash their robes')
the faithful given the tree of life and the holy city

schools.summary: The excluded sinners: 'for without are dogs, and sorcerers, and whoremongers, and murderers, and idolaters, and whosoever loveth and maketh a lie' — outside the city the excluded (dogs, sorcerers, fornicators, murderers, idolaters, and all who love and practice falsehood, the catalogue of the unredeemed who have no part in the holy city, cf. 21:8); 'dogs' (a figure of the impure, shameless, or those outside the covenant, Philippians 3:2; Deuteronomy 23:18, the term marking the morally unclean and excluded, the dogs without, shut out of the city); 'sorcerers, and whoremongers, and murderers, and idolaters, and whosoever loveth and maketh a lie' (as 21:8, the catalogue of those excluded from the city, 'whosoever loveth and maketh a lie' emphasizing that all falsehood is shut out, the holy city admitting nothing false or unclean) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: For without are dogs, and sorcerers, and whoremongers, and murderers, and idolaters, and whosoever loveth and maketh a lie
the excluded sinners outside the city
the catalogue of the unredeemed who have no part in the holy city
all unclean and false shut out

schools.summary: Jesus's testimony and titles: 'I Jesus have sent mine angel to testify unto you these things in the churches. I am the root and the offspring of David, and the bright and morning star' — Jesus himself attesting that he sent his angel to give this testimony to the churches (the revelation Christ's own, sent through his angel, 1:1, the personal seal of the Lord on the whole book); 'the root and the offspring of David' (both the source/root and the descendant/offspring of David, 5:5; Isaiah 11:1, 10; Matthew 22:42-45, he David's Lord and David's son, the Messiah of David's line yet David's origin, the fulfillment of the Davidic promise); 'the bright and morning star' (2:28; Numbers 24:17; 2 Peter 1:19, the herald of the dawn, the bringer of light and the new day, the morning star ushering in the eternal day, the figure of Christ as the light and hope of his people, the radiant harbinger of glory) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: I Jesus have sent mine angel to testify unto you these things in the churches
I am the root and the offspring of David, and the bright and morning star
Jesus's testimony and his titles
Christ the Messiah and the herald of the eternal dawn

schools.summary: The great invitation: 'and the Spirit and the bride say, Come. And let him that heareth say, Come. And let him that is athirst come. And whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely' — the Holy Spirit and the bride (the church) together saying 'Come' (the invitation to come to Christ, or for Christ to come, the Spirit and the church joining in calling sinners to the water of life and/or in longing for the Lord's coming); 'let him that heareth say, Come. And let him that is athirst come' (everyone who hears to join the invitation and to come, the thirsty bidden to come, the call widening from the Spirit and bride to every hearer and every thirsty soul, the universal gospel invitation); 'whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely' (the invitation open to all who will, the water of life, 21:6; Isaiah 55:1; John 7:37, offered freely without price, the gracious, universal, free offer of salvation, whoever desires may come and take the water of life) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the Spirit and the bride say, Come. And let him that heareth say, Come
whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely
the great invitation of the Spirit and the bride
the gracious, free, universal invitation to salvation

schools.summary: The warning against adding: 'for I testify unto every man that heareth the words of the prophecy of this book, If any man shall add unto these things, God shall add unto him the plagues that are written in this book' — a solemn testimony (Christ's, or John's) given to every hearer of the prophecy (the warning that follows binding all who hear the book, the words not to be tampered with); 'if any man shall add unto these things' (to add to the words of the prophecy, Deuteronomy 4:2; 12:32, forbidden, whether of this book specifically or by extension of the sacred word, the integrity of the prophecy to be guarded, nothing to be added); 'God shall add unto him the plagues that are written in this book' (the one who adds having the plagues of the book added to him, the solemn penalty for tampering with the prophecy, the judgment threatened on those who corrupt the sacred word, the warning guarding the book's integrity) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: If any man shall add unto these things, God shall add unto him the plagues that are written in this book
the warning against adding to the prophecy
the integrity of the prophecy guarded against addition
the penalty for tampering with the sacred word

schools.summary: The warning against taking away: 'and if any man shall take away from the words of the book of this prophecy, God shall take away his part out of the book of life, and out of the holy city, and from the things which are written in this book' — to take away from (subtract, omit, or remove) the words of the prophecy forbidden, as adding was (v.18, the integrity of the book guarded against both addition and subtraction, Deuteronomy 4:2); 'God shall take away his part out of the book of life, and out of the holy city, and from the things which are written in this book' (the one who takes away having his part taken from the book of life, the holy city, and the blessings of the book, the loss of salvation, the city, and the promises, the solemn penalty for corrupting the prophecy by subtraction); the twofold warning (vv.18-19) solemnly guarding the integrity of the prophecy (neither addition nor subtraction permitted, the sacred word to be preserved entire, the judgment threatened protecting the book's purity) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: if any man shall take away from the words of the book of this prophecy, God shall take away his part out of the book of life, and out of the holy city
the warning against taking from the prophecy
the integrity of the prophecy guarded against subtraction
the penalty of forfeited salvation

schools.summary: Christ's final promise and the church's prayer: 'he which testifieth these things saith, Surely I come quickly. Amen. Even so, come, Lord Jesus' — Christ, who testifies (v.16), giving his final promise: 'Surely (yea) I come quickly' (the third and last of the chapter, vv.7, 12, 20, the closing assurance of his swift, certain coming, the great hope of the church sealed by Christ's own word, the emphatic certain promise, the last word of the risen Lord in Scripture); 'Amen. Even so, come, Lord Jesus' the answering prayer (John and the church answering with 'Amen' — so be it — and the prayer 'Even so, come, Lord Jesus,' Maranatha, 1 Corinthians 16:22, the believing, longing response to Christ's promise, the church echoing his word with the prayer for his coming, the heart of the bride answering the Bridegroom) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: He which testifieth these things saith, Surely I come quickly. Amen. Even so, come, Lord Jesus
Christ's final promise and the church's answering prayer
Christ's assured coming and the bride's longing prayer
Amen, even so, come, Lord Jesus (Maranatha)

schools.summary: The closing benediction: 'the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen' — the book (and the whole canon of Scripture) closing with a benediction of grace (the apostolic blessing, as in Paul's letters, e.g. Romans 16:20; 2 Corinthians 13:14, invoking the grace of Christ upon all the readers, the prophecy of judgment and glory ending in grace); 'the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ' (the unmerited favor and saving kindness of Christ invoked, the book that has unfolded the judgments and the glory closing with the grace of Christ, the ground of all the redeemed enjoy, grace the last word); 'be with you all. Amen' (the grace of Christ invoked upon all, the closing 'Amen' — so be it — sealing the benediction and the whole book, the Bible ending with the prayer of grace and the church's 'Amen,' the fitting close of the Scriptures in the grace of the Lord Jesus) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Cyril: the book closes with the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ invoked upon all, sealed with 'Amen' — the grace of Christ the final word of the prophecy and of all Scripture, the saving favor of the Lord upon his people; the fitting close of the revelation of judgment and glory in the grace of the Lord Jesus, the church answering 'Amen' to the benediction of grace.

themes: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen
the closing benediction of grace
the book and the canon of Scripture closing in the grace of Christ
grace the last word, sealed with the church's Amen
